title: The Salt-Memory Convergence

features.name: estuary-settlement
features.desc-on-touch: A cluster of salt-weathered buildings where the alphabet came to die into the sea. You walk between structures that remember being readable, before salt rewrote their walls.

features.name: salt-repository-shed
features.desc-on-touch: A warehouse where salt once lay in tons, waiting for preservation or trade. Now the archive stores the memory: how salt tastes like time, how preserving something means erasing its story.

features.name: crystallization-pool
features.desc-on-touch: Where the drowned alphabet crystallizes into salt formations. Letters become mineral become unreadable. The corruption loves here: writing that transforms into chemical memory.

features.name: tidal-archive-mark
features.desc-on-touch: A tide-line carved into rock, showing where the estuary rose and fell for decades. Each line a season's high point. The archive reads it as a record of patience and repetition.

features.name: maritime-register-house
features.desc-on-touch: Where ships' logs were kept and salt spray gradually erased their entries. The archive found the blank pages themselves became more legible than what was written.

features.name: divergence-monument
features.desc-on-touch: A salt-crusted stone where the drowned alphabet emerges from freshwater to meet the sea. Language itself splits here: some words wash away, others crystallize into permanence.

inscription: salt and water meet at the estuary; memory crystallizes where two forms of forgetting converge

file.name: Estuary_Settlement_Oral_History_SaltDamaged_Transcript.txt
file.note: Recorded interviews from the estuary town, degraded by salt-air corruption. Words randomly become 'salt' or 'water' mid-sentence, as if the environment itself rewrites testimony. The speakers seem not

exits.label: back south to the drowned alphabet where freshwater still remembers words
east to the tidal flats where salt meets open sea
west along the estuary to older settlements not yet erased
